title: Why Balanced Scaling Helps
concept: Balanced scaling helps because when only one axis grows, another part of the CNN can become the bottleneck, limiting how much value the larger model gets from the extra compute.

exercises.prompt: Recap: if a CNN sees detailed images but has too few channels to describe many feature types, which axis is most obviously limited?
exercises.explain: Right — and that is the broader lesson: whichever weak axis stays small can become the bottleneck.

cards.title: A bigger bucket does not fix a narrow pipe
cards.body: Suppose water flows through a narrow pipe into a huge storage tank. Making the tank bigger does not solve the real limit: the pipe. CNNs behave similarly. If one scaling axis stays too small, extra growth somewhere else can be underused.

cards.title: What does 'bottleneck' mean here?
cards.body: In scaling, a bottleneck is the part of the model that limits how useful the rest can be. A very deep network with tiny channels may lack enough feature capacity. A wide network on blurry tiny inputs may have lots of channels but too little detail to analyze. Balanced scaling tries to reduce these mismatches.

cards.title: Race car with one weak part
cards.body: A race car with a stronger engine still loses if the tires cannot grip the road. Upgrading only one component can leave the overall system constrained by another. EfficientNet applies that systems-thinking mindset to CNN growth.

cards.title: Small defect detection
cards.body: Imagine a factory-inspection CNN looking for hairline cracks. If you only add depth but keep low-resolution inputs, the deeper network still may not see enough crack detail. If you only raise resolution but keep the network narrow, the model may receive more detail than it can represent well.

cards.title: More compute is not automatically well-spent
cards.body: It is tempting to think any increase in model size helps equally. In reality, added compute can be wasted if it is poured into the wrong axis while another axis remains the true limit. EfficientNet's insight is about spending budget where growth supports growth.

cards.title: Where the bottleneck shows up
cards.body: Single-axis growth can leave the rest of the system lagging behind.

left.text:
One axis grows a lot
Another axis stays cramped
Extra capacity gets underused

right.text:
Multiple axes grow together
Fewer obvious choke points
Compute supports the full pipeline

exercises.prompt: Why might making a CNN much wider not help as much as expected if input resolution stays very low?

options.text: Because the model may have more channels than the coarse input detail can justify
Because width automatically deletes the convolution filters
Because low resolution prevents all backpropagation
Because channels only matter in language models

exercises.explain: Exactly — if the input remains too coarse, extra channels may not unlock much value.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Balanced scaling tries to reduce situations where one part of the network outgrows what another part can support.
exercises.explain: True — that is the heart of the bottleneck idea.

exercises.sentence: If one scaling axis grows while another stays weak, the weak one can become the ___.
exercises.explain: Right — the bottleneck is the limiting part of the system.

exercises.prompt: Which statement shows the logic of balanced scaling?

options.text: When several useful parts of the pipeline grow together, extra compute is less likely to get trapped behind one weak link
As long as one axis grows dramatically, the others no longer matter
Balanced scaling means every layer must have identical weights

exercises.explain: Yes — EfficientNet is about reducing weak-link waste, not about making everything identical.

exercises.prompt: A team doubles image resolution for a defect-detection model but keeps the network tiny and narrow. The new images contain more detail, yet gains are small. What is the best explanation?

options.text: Resolution grew, but model capacity did not grow enough to use the extra detail well, so another bottleneck remained
Higher resolution always lowers vision accuracy, no matter the architecture
Once resolution increases, depth and width become irrelevant
The problem proves CNNs cannot use high-resolution images at all

exercises.explain: Exactly. One stronger axis cannot guarantee big gains if another axis stays limiting. Next, we will look at the EfficientNet family, where B0, B1, B2, and beyond are scaled versions of the same base idea.
